# OWID Caption Prompt — V4 vs V8 (current best)

5 chart images comparing baseline V4 vs V8 (the strongest version).

**V4** (Sept iteration #4): tight ~160-word data-story, no layout, no note rule.
**V8** (current): title + subtitle layout, ~274 words, verbatim-grounded note transcription, hedge-tightened.

Look for: (1) layout structure in V8, (2) note text incorporated where present, (3) more named examples in V8.

---

## Infectious and parasitic diseases death rate
`infectious-and-parasitic-diseases-death-rate-who-mdb`

**Chart's Note line (printed beneath the chart):** To allow for comparisons between countries and over time, this metric is [age-standardized](#dod:age_standardized). All deaths in a country may not have been registered with a [cause of death](#dod:underlying-cause-of-death).

<img src="data:image/png;base64," width="700" />

### V4 baseline (137w)

Across seven countries from 1950 to 2023, the reported annual death rate from infectious and parasitic diseases per 100,000 people shows divergent trajectories. South Africa’s line begins in the mid-1990s near 120, climbs steeply to a peak just under 400 around 2006, then falls to approximately 130 by 2023. Brazil’s rate starts above 60 in 1980, declines gradually to below 30 by 2023. The United States, France, Germany, and Australia all remain below 20 throughout their visible ranges, with Australia’s line staying closest to zero after 1970. Russia’s line, beginning in the early 1980s near 25, rises slightly through the 1990s before settling back toward 20 by 2023. The most prominent feature is South Africa’s sharp rise and fall between 1995 and 2023, reaching nearly four times the height of any other country’s peak during that period.

### V8 current best (250w)

Infectious and parasitic diseases death rate, 1950 to 2023

The reported annual death rate from infectious and parasitic diseases per 100,000 people, based on the underlying cause listed on death certificates.

This line chart tracks the reported annual death rate from infectious and parasitic diseases per 100,000 people across six nations from 1950 to 2023, with the metric age-standardized to allow for comparisons between countries and over time. The vertical axis spans from 0 to 400 deaths per 100,000 people. South Africa displays a distinct trajectory compared to the other nations, with data beginning around 1997 at approximately 125 deaths per 100,000. The line for South Africa rises steeply to a peak near 390 around 2006, followed by a consistent decline to roughly 130 by 2023. In contrast, the United States shows a high starting point in 1950 near 85 deaths per 100,000, dropping rapidly to below 20 by 1970 and remaining flat near 10 through 2023. France and Germany follow similar downward paths, starting near 40 in 1950 and settling below 15 for the remainder of the timeline. Brazil begins its recorded data in 1980 around 65 deaths per 100,000, decreasing gradually to the 25-30 range by 2023. Russia and Australia maintain the lowest visible rates, with Russia hovering between 20 and 30 after 1980 and Australia staying consistently below 10. The data shows that while most countries experienced a long-term reduction in mortality from these causes, South Africa underwent a severe temporary increase before returning to lower levels.

---

## Rate of child deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition
`rate-of-child-deaths-attributed-to-child-and-maternal-malnutrition`

*(no Note line on this chart)*

<img src="data:image/png;base64," width="700" />

### V4 baseline (161w)

Across the globe in 2023, the rate of child deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition varies significantly by region, measured as the estimated number of deaths per 100,000 children under the age of 5. The highest rates, falling in the darkest red bands representing over 1,000 deaths per 100,000, are concentrated in a contiguous belt across sub-Saharan Africa, including nations such as Nigeria, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, and Somalia. Moderate rates, visible in the orange and lighter red shades corresponding to 50 to 500 deaths per 100,000, appear across parts of South Asia, including India and Pakistan, as well as in pockets of Central America and the Middle East. Conversely, the lightest beige colors, indicating rates below 10 deaths per 100,000, cover the majority of North America, Europe, Australia, and the southern cone of South America. The geographic distribution shows a clear concentration of high mortality rates in the African continent compared to the rest of the world.

### V8 current best (238w)

Rate of child deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition, 2023
The estimated number of deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition, per 100,000 children under the age of 5.

This choropleth map displays the estimated number of deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition per 100,000 children under the age of 5 across the globe in 2023. The color scale ranges from light beige, representing rates near zero, to deep maroon, indicating rates exceeding 2,000 deaths per 100,000 children. The highest concentrations of mortality appear in a broad band across sub-Saharan Africa, where countries such as Somalia, Chad, the Central African Republic, and Niger fall into the darkest red category, signifying rates above 2,000. Surrounding nations including Mali, Burkina Faso, and South Sudan also show very high rates, generally falling within the 500 to 2,000 range. In Asia, Afghanistan and Yemen display elevated rates in the 200 to 500 band, while India and Pakistan show moderate levels between 50 and 200. Conversely, North America, Europe, Australia, and most of South America appear in the lightest shades, indicating rates below 10 deaths per 100,000 children. The data source is the Institute for Health Metrics and Evaluation (IHME), Global Burden of Disease (2025). The visualization shows a distinct geographic disparity, with the burden of malnutrition-related child mortality concentrated heavily in specific regions of Africa and parts of Asia, while remaining minimal in high-income regions and much of the Americas.

---

## Cumulative number of objects launched into space
`cumulative-number-of-objects-launched-into-outer-space`

**Chart's Note line (printed beneath the chart):** When an object is launched by a country on behalf of another one, it is attributed to the latter.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," width="700" />

### V4 baseline (145w)

From 1957 through 2025, the cumulative number of objects launched into Earth orbit or beyond has risen from zero to approximately 25,000. The World line shows a steady increase for the first four decades, followed by a sharp acceleration in the 2010s and a near-vertical climb after 2020. The United States line follows a similar trajectory, remaining below the global total but rising steeply in recent years to reach roughly 16,000. Russia’s line climbs more gradually, plateauing around 4,000 after 2000. China’s line remains flat until the 2000s, then rises to about 2,000 by 2025. Japan, France, India, Germany, and the United Kingdom each show modest growth, with all staying under 1,000 launches. The European Space Agency line begins in the 1980s and reaches just over 1,000 by 2025. The steepest visible rise occurs in the World and United States lines between 2020 and 2025.

### V8 current best (209w)

Cumulative number of objects launched into space
This includes satellites, probes, landers, crewed spacecrafts, and space station flight elements launched into Earth orbit or beyond.

The line chart tracks the total accumulation of space objects launched from 1957 through 2025, measuring the count on a vertical axis that extends to 25,000. The data aggregates satellites, probes, landers, crewed spacecrafts, and space station flight elements, with attribution assigned to the country on whose behalf the object is launched. The World total line begins at zero in 1957 and rises steadily to approximately 5,000 by the year 2000, before accelerating sharply to reach 25,000 by the end of the timeline. The United States line follows a similar trajectory, remaining below 2,500 until roughly 2010, then climbing steeply to surpass 15,000. Russia maintains a position as the third-highest launcher, with its cumulative total reaching just under 5,000 by 2025. China shows a distinct upward curve starting around 2000, rising to approximately 2,000 objects. The United Kingdom, Japan, France, India, Germany, and the European Space Agency occupy the lower portion of the chart, with all individual totals remaining below 1,000. The visual pattern displays a long period of gradual accumulation followed by a rapid expansion in launch frequency across the most recent decade.

---

## Mental health and substance use disorders death rate by age group
`death-rates-from-mental-and-substance-disorders-by-age-who`

*(no Note line on this chart)*

<img src="data:image/png;base64," width="700" />

### V4 baseline (117w)

In 2021, the estimated annual number of deaths from mental health and substance use disorders per 100,000 people varied significantly across age groups. The death rate for the 50-69 age group was the highest at 9.2, followed by the 70+ age group at 6.9. The 15-49 age group had a death rate of 4.5, while the overall rate for all ages was 4.4. Death rates for younger age groups were notably lower, with the 5-14 age group at less than 0.1 and the 0-4 age group at 0. The data shows a clear pattern where death rates from these disorders increase with age, peaking in the 50-69 age group before declining slightly in the oldest age category.

### V8 current best (258w)

Mental health and substance use disorders death rate by age group, World, 2021

Estimated annual number of deaths by age group from mental health and substance use disorders per 100,000 people in the same age group. These include alcohol use, drug use, and eating disorders. Figures do not include deaths resulting from suicide, which are related to these disorders.

This horizontal bar chart displays the estimated annual death rate from mental health and substance use disorders per 100,000 people across six distinct age groups globally in 2021. The data encompasses deaths related to alcohol use, drug use, and eating disorders, while explicitly excluding deaths resulting from suicide. The vertical axis lists the age categories, ranging from early childhood to the elderly, while the horizontal bars represent the mortality rate values. The distribution shows a clear correlation between advancing age and higher mortality rates from these specific causes. The 50-69 years age group records the highest death rate at 9.2 per 100,000 people. The 70+ years category follows with the second-highest rate of 6.9. Moving to younger demographics, the 15-49 years group shows a rate of 4.5, which is nearly identical to the global average for all ages combined, recorded at 4.4. The rates drop significantly for children; the 5-14 years group has a rate of less than 0.1, and the 0-4 years group shows a value of 0. The visual arrangement highlights that mortality from these disorders is concentrated in the adult and elderly populations, with the 50-69 age bracket representing the peak value among all displayed categories.

---

## Financial support provided through the Green Climate Fund
`green-climate-gcf-fund-pledges`

**Chart's Note line (printed beneath the chart):** This is measured in current US dollars per year.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," width="700" />

### V4 baseline (130w)

Between 2011 and 2022, the total financial support provided through the Green Climate Fund for climate change mitigation and adaptation shows a general upward trajectory. The line begins in 2011 at just under $30 billion and remains flat through 2012. From 2012 to 2016, the value rises steadily to approximately $52 billion. A slight dip occurs in 2017, followed by a recovery in 2018 to roughly $56 billion. The data plateaus between 2019 and 2020, hovering near $53 billion. From 2020 to 2022, the line exhibits its steepest incline, climbing past $60 billion and $70 billion to reach a peak of $80 billion in the final year. The chart records the highest value in 2022, which is more than double the funding level observed at the start of the period.

### V8 current best (259w)

Financial support provided through the Green Climate Fund, 2011 to 2022
The total financial support provided to developing countries for climate change mitigation and adaptation. Countries have set a collective goal of mobilizing $100 billion per year from 2020 onwards.

The line chart tracks the total financial support provided to developing countries for climate change mitigation and adaptation from 2011 to 2022, measured in current US dollars per year. The vertical axis ranges from $0 to $80 billion, while the horizontal axis marks the years. The data shows a general upward trend over the period, starting at approximately $29 billion in 2011 and 2012. A significant increase occurs between 2012 and 2013, where the value rises to just over $40 billion. The line continues to climb steadily, reaching roughly $51 billion in 2016 before a slight dip in 2017 to just under $50 billion. Following a recovery in 2018 to about $55 billion, the values plateau slightly through 2019 and 2020, hovering near the $53 billion mark. From 2020 onwards, the chart displays a steep acceleration, with the line rising sharply to cross $60 billion in 2021 and reaching a peak of $80 billion in 2022. The metric is measured in current US dollars per year. The highest value recorded on the chart is the 2022 data point at $80 billion, which remains below the collective goal of $100 billion per year mentioned in the subtitle. The most prominent feature of the visualization is the rapid growth in funding volume during the final two years of the observed period.

---
